tldr: Headlines split sharply between two registers: fatal-encounter reporting (ICE agents shoot drivers, growing death toll, officer's violent past) and policy-reversal reporting (Trump overrides DHS suspension, traffic stops resume). India and UK outlets fixate on the individual shooter's troubled history, China leads with the bare fact of death, and The Intercept is the only outlet to surface the story's electoral dimension in a headline. Spain's El Pais alone foregrounds community grief in Biddeford.

frames.sub_frame: community grief and lasting civic wound

evidence.quote: Biddeford mourns as ICE leaves its mark on yet another US city

single_outlet_findings.finding: The only headline to frame ICE's own communication as the subject — 'What ICE Didn't Say About Its Latest Killing' — making institutional silence and omission the advertised story rather than the shooting itself.

The only headline to frame resumed traffic stops as a positive procedural update ('Are Back, But There Are Some Big Changes'), reversing the dominant victim-centered register and positioning the policy resumption as reform rather than escalation.

The only headline to foreground civic grief and place identity ('Biddeford mourns … yet another US city'), a QUALITY_OF_LIFE frame entirely absent from every other outlet in the corpus at the headline level.

Only outlet to link the shootings to Maine electoral politics — 'Would-Be Platner Replacements in Maine Rally Around Abolish ICE' — surfacing the story's downstream political mobilization as the lead hook, invisible in all other headlines.

bottom_line: At the headline layer, the ICE traffic-stop shootings story bifurcates into two signals — 'ICE killed men in their cars' (China, Spain, UK, and some India/US outlets) and 'Trump reversed the pause' (Brazil, Vietnam/Malaysia, Canada, and the US policy press) — with the officer's personal history of violence dominating India and UK headlines and community grief in Biddeford appearing only in Spain's El Pais.
